abstract: Radical cross-coupling has emerged as a transformative strategy for constructing C(sp3)–C and C(sp3)–heteroatom bonds, typically through the synergy of transition-metal catalysis with photoredox or electrochemical activation. In contrast, the potential for main-group elements to mediate similar radical-based transformations remains largely untapped. Here, we demonstrate that a low-valent bismuth complex can undergo one-electron oxidative addition with redox-active alkyl radical precursors, effectively mimicking the reactivity of first-row transition metals. This approach facilitates the isolation and full characterization of well-defined Bi(III)–C(sp3) oxidative addition complexes in both solution and the solid state. We observed that these intermediates exhibit divergent reactivity patterns dictated by the electronic nature of the alkyl α-substituents. Leveraging these mechanistic insights, we developed a bismuth-catalyzed C(sp3)–N cross-coupling protocol. This method operates under mild conditions and enables the efficient coupling of a wide range of synthetically valuable NH-heterocycles.
